narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, your LSAT score of 31, your Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings of 1 and 2 respectively, and details like your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is notably higher at 37.27, and decile rankings tend to cluster much higher, around 6 for both early and later years. The three biggest factors shaping this prediction were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 1 decile, and your Year 3 decile. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's above the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it nudged the model's assessment toward expecting success rather than failure. In contrast, your lower Year 1 decile ranking pushed strongly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar struggles compared to typical admitted students who ranked much higher in their cohorts. Similarly, your Year 3 decile had a comparable negative pull, reinforcing concerns about sustained performance relative to those who passed. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of bar passers (about 45%), and full-time status is common among them (over 93%), these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by having strengths in GPA and background but falling short in early deciles and LSAT, tipping the balance toward predicted failure. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37+ range that successful students typically achieve, and reflect on strategies to improve your class rankings in future coursework. Building a stronger narrative around your academic trajectory through additional recommendations or targeted prep could also help in reapplying. With focused effort on these areas, you can shift your predicted odds and strengthen your candidacy next time.
